headline: Urban Heat Island Effect Raises KL Temperatures by 4°C
context: A 2025 UKM study using 180 sensor stations found that central Kuala Lumpur is consistently 4°C hotter than surrounding suburbs due to the urban heat island effect.

cards.big: KL's average temperature has risen 2.1°C since 1980.
cards.sub: Half of that increase is from global warming. The other half is from concrete, asphalt, and lost tree cover.

cards.big: KL's green cover fell from 42% in 2000 to 27% in 2024.
cards.sub: DBKL's own target was 30% minimum. The city breached its own floor in 2021 without public disclosure.

cards.big: Outdoor workers in KL face heat stress 147 days per year.
cards.sub: OSHA guidelines recommend work-rest cycles above 33°C. Compliance monitoring covers only 8% of construction sites.

cards.big: Air conditioning costs consume 23% of average KL household electricity bills.
cards.sub: For B40 households, this represents RM1,800 annually — a direct poverty tax from heat.

cards.big: Heat is not weather. In KL, it is an infrastructure failure with a postcode.
Urban heat is a solvable engineering problem — tree canopy, reflective surfaces, green corridors. KL has a plan on paper. It needs a plan with a budget.
